BTN ecology

Sampling sites. Areas surveyed only in 2021 are marked in white, only in 2023 in yellow, and in both years in red.

The frequency distribution of BTN1 and BTN2.

The prevalence of BTN1 in samples averages 2.07%, BTN2 – 2.01 %.

PRevalence of BTN1 and BTN2 along shore

Symbol size is proportional to BTN prevalence, with data for BTN1 and BTN2 presented separately.

Mussel population structure

Ordination of population parameters in PCA axes.

Ordination of all sites in PCA axes.

In the analysis, L3-L58 is the abundance of mussel size classes in quantitative samples at each sampling site. (At each site 2-3 samples were taken for mussel size structure analysis). Biomass is the biomass at the entire site, taking into account the cover of mussels.

Before calculating PCA, all variables were standardized (mean = 0, sd = 1).

PCA interpretation

PC1 reflects a gradient from sparse mussel beds dominated by large molluscs with length 38-58 mm to populations with abundant young mussels, high projective cover and high biomass.

The same figure, but only the populations studied in 2021 and 2023 are shown. Note that, with the exception of the “Khol” site, all populations have shifted from left to right along the PC1 axis. That is, the populations have become, on average, younger over the two years. Below are box plots of PC1 values for the same sites in different years.

Thus PC1 reflecs the temporal changes in mussel population structure: from old-dominated settlements to young-dominated ones.

Size structure and abundance of mussels at study sites. Blue rug on axes mark the sizes of mussels analyzed for BTN presence.

The size structures of mussels in samples are shown here, with sites ordered by increasing PC1 value (down each column).

Relationship between BTN prevalence and environmental predictors.

Predictors were the next.

  • Fetch, openness of the shore to the surf. Technically - unobstructed length of water surface (km) over which wind from a certain direction can blow.
  • Dist_port - distance from the harbor measured along the shoreline.
  • PC1 (see above)
  • OGP, overall growth performanse. OGP = log (L∞ ∗ K), where L∞ and K – parameters of the vonBertalanffy equation calculated from the sizes of the year rings on mussel shells. To estimate OGP for a site, a few dozen of biggest mussels were studied and individual OGP values were averaged.

Salinity was not included sinse did not vary netween sites.

Row data in gradients of predictors

BTN1 prevalence exhibited a nonlinear association with PC1, characterized by a decreasing trend over the range of PC1 values. In contrast, no association was observed between BTN2 prevalence and PC1.

Prevalence of two BTN lineages across repeatedly sampled sites (2021–2023).

On average, across the six resampled sites, the frequency of BTN1 decreased between 2021 and 2023. This again support the idea about temporal changes reflecting by PC1. No clear temporal pattern was observed for BTN2

BTN Prevalence in Relation to Along-Shore Distance from Magadan Sea Port

BTN1 prevalence shows a clear increasing trend, while BTN2 exhibits no clear pattern.

Relationship Between BTN Prevalence and Fetch, a Proxy for Wave Action

BTN1 prevalence increases distinctly, while BTN2 shows no clear trend.

BTN Prevalence Versus OGP (a Proxy for Site-Specific Mussel Growth Rate)

Both lineages show a positive association with mussel growth rate.

Modelling the dependencies of BTN prevalences on predictors

Generalized Additive Mixed Model (GAMs) was fitted using the ‘mgcv’ package in R to assess the relationships between BTN prevalence and the predictors under consideration. “Site” and “Year” were included in the model as random factors.

Mod <- gam(cbind(N_cancer, N_helthy) ~ 
             s((Dist_Port), by = Lineage,  bs = "cs", k = 5) + 
             s((fetch), by = Lineage,  bs = "cs", k = 5) +  
             s((PC1), by = Lineage,  bs = "cs", k = 5) + 
             s((OGP), by = Lineage, bs = "cs", k = 5) +  
             Lineage +  
             s(Year, Site, bs = "re"),  
           family = "binomial", 
           method = "REML", 
           data = cancer_21_23_predictors )
Summary of the generalized additive mixed model (GAMM) assessing the effects of predictors on BTN prevalence

Significant associations were found for BTN1 with distance to port, fetch, and PC1, while no significant patterns emerged for BTN2. However, BTN2 was significantly related to OGP, with BTN1 showing only a marginal relationship.

Model visualization

Relationship between the frequency of two BTN lineages and distance to port. Partial effects show the effect of a predictor after accounting for all other predictors in the model. The horizontal line represents the mean; positive values indicate above-mean effects, negative values below-mean effects. Points are partial residuals.

Relationship between the frequency of two BTN lineages and fetch.

Relationship between the frequency of two BTN lineages and PC1 reflecting variation in mussels population structure.

Relationship between the frequency of two BTN lineages and OGP reflecting variation in mussels growth rate.

Consequences of BTN for the host

Gonad development is often found to be disrupted in BTN-infected mussels.

Frequency of gonad development impairment versus disease stage

To investigate the effect of aneuploidy on gonad condition across different BTN lineages, we fitted a generalized additive model (GAM). The model included a smooth term for the proportion of aneuploid cells (s(Aneuploid_Proportion)), which was allowed to vary by BTN lineage (using the by = Lineage argument). Lineage was also included as a parametric factor. The model was fited with a binomial family to account for the binary nature of the gonad outcome variable.

Mod_gonad <- gam(Gonad_Out ~ s(Aneuploid_Proportion, by = Lineage) + 
                   Lineage , 
                 family = "binomial",  
                 method = "REML", 
                 data = myt_ind_clean_not_healthy)
Параметры GAM

Visible impairments emerge at later stages in BTN1-infected individuals, whereas in BTN2 infections, they are evident from the early stages onward.

Effect of BTN on mollusk growth

Scheme of shell growth measurement. Last ring size was used as covariate in statistic models.

To examine the effect of BTN infection on final-year shell growth, we fitted a generalized additive model (GAM). The model included BTN infection status (BTN_Type) as a parametric factor and a cubic regression smooth term for the last annual ring (s(Last_ring, bs = “cr”)), which was allowed to vary by infection status (using the by = BTN_Type argument). To account for repeated sampling and potential individual-level variation, a random effect smooth term for individual sample ID (s(Sample, bs = “re”)) was included. The model was fit with a Gaussian family.

Mod_prop_incr_btn <- gam(Fi_Increment ~ BTN_Type + 
                           s(Last_ring, bs = "cr", by = BTN_Type)  + 
                           s(Sample, bs = "re"), 
                         family = "gaussian", 
                         data = myt_ind_clean, method = "REML")

The dependent variable Fi_Increment is \(\varphi\)-transposed relative growth (RL).
